narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.60, which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, along with an LSAT score of 24—noticeably lower than the average of about 37. You're ranked in the top decile for your Year 1 school grades but third decile for Year 3, come from a lower-middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. The model's assessment gives you a 48% predicted probability of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes those with stronger signals of first-time bar passage. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which stood out as a major concern compared to typical students because it was well below average. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA played the strongest role in countering that, pulling the prediction toward success since it's competitively close to the norm despite not being top-tier. Your family income background also helped significantly, as coming from a lower-middle bracket has historically aligned with resilience and bar passage in our data. Being a full-time student further nudged things positively, matching the successful profile of most who pass. Your Year 1 and Year 3 deciles had smaller influences, with the top Year 1 rank slightly favoring passage and the Year 3 rank mildly contributing to concerns. While this decision reflects patterns from past students, it doesn't define your potential—many in similar circumstances have succeeded by targeting improvements. I encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost that key score, strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and seek mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout reapplication next cycle. You've got this; persistence like yours changes outcomes.
